notes.note: The fanum was the consecrated shrine Cicero planned to build to his dead daughter Tullia, less a tomb than a memorial that would amount to her deification (apotheosis). The whole sequence of Astura letters revolves around settling its design, its site, and its financing.
Cluatius was the architect whose plan for Tullia's shrine Cicero had accepted; here Cicero says he is settled on the design (genus) and the principle, and wavers only on the location (locus).

notes.anchor: I shall surely consecrate her with every kind of memorial
notes.note: To "consecrate" (consecrare) Tullia is to enrol her among the divine; Cicero defends the unusual ambition by citing Greek and Latin authorities for honouring the dead in this way. This is the apotheosis project that troubled even Atticus.

notes.anchor: For my own principal I trust Sulpicius
notes.note: The "principal" (sors) is the capital sum of a loan as distinct from its interest; Cicero says he trusts Servius Sulpicius Rufus and the banker Egnatius to make the capital good. Sulpicius here is the eminent jurist, not any namesake.
